question_title: Which of the following will best match Copernicus’s explicit wording about March 2026 Arctic sea ice in its March 2026 climate bulletin before April 30, 2026?
summary:
The Copernicus March 2026 climate bulletin hasn't been published yet (expected April 7-10 based on past timing). March 2026 Arctic sea ice was at or near record-low levels: the annual maximum extent (14.29 million km²) was technically the lowest on record, though NSIDC declared it "statistically tied" with 2025's 14.31 million km². The monthly mean extent is unknown but likely very close to March 2025's record of 14.2 million km².

Copernicus consistently provides explicit ordinal rankings for noteworthy months using clean language ("lowest", "second-lowest", "third lowest"), making "No explicit rank" very unlikely. The key uncertainty is whether Copernicus calls any metric "lowest on record" (which depends on their OSI SAF dataset), uses "tied" language (resolving to "Other"), or calls both monthly mean and maximum "second-lowest."

Multiple metrics discussed in the bulletin (monthly mean, annual maximum, daily rankings) create multiple opportunities for "lowest" to appear. However, the "tied" framing from NSIDC could lead Copernicus to qualified language. OSI SAF mid-March data showed daily extent as "second lowest for the date" behind 2025, suggesting the monthly mean may end up second-lowest.

factors.description: March 2026 annual maximum extent was technically lowest on record (14.29 vs 14.31 million km² in 2025), though NSIDC calls them 'statistically tied'. If Copernicus's OSI SAF data shows the same ordering, they may explicitly call it 'lowest annual maximum'.
Copernicus consistently provides explicit ordinal rankings in their bulletins for noteworthy months. March 2026 is clearly noteworthy, making 'No explicit rank' very unlikely.
factors.supports: No explicit rank or classification stated

factors.description: OSI SAF data (Copernicus's data source) showed March 12 daily extent as 'second lowest for the date' behind 2025, suggesting the monthly mean may be second-lowest rather than lowest.
NSIDC used 'tied' language for the maximum, and Copernicus might follow suit. 'Tied for lowest' would resolve as 'Other explicit rank or classification'.
Copernicus historically uses clean ordinal ranks rather than 'tied' language, reducing the probability of 'Other' from tied wording.

Multiple metrics are discussed in Copernicus bulletins (monthly mean, annual maximum, daily rankings). If ANY metric is called 'lowest on record', the question resolves there, giving multiple chances.
Sea ice VOLUME in March 2026 was unambiguously the lowest on record per DMI/ICCI data. If the bulletin mentions volume, it would be 'lowest on record'.
factors.conditional: If Copernicus discusses sea ice volume in their climate bulletin

factors.description: 2026 maximum reached 5 days earlier than 2025 (March 15 vs 20), meaning more of March was in decline phase, potentially pulling the monthly mean below 2025's record.

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary:**

| Option | Probability |
|--------|------------|
| Lowest on record | **45%** |
| Second-lowest on record | **22%** |
| Other explicit rank or classification | **26%** |
| No explicit rank or classification stated | **7%** |

**Key reasoning:**

The Copernicus March 2026 bulletin is expected around April 7-10 (well before the April 30 deadline). March 2026 Arctic sea ice was at or near record-low levels — the annual maximum was technically the lowest ever at 14.29 million km² (vs 2025's 14.31), though NSIDC declared them "statistically tied."

- **"Lowest" gets 45%** because the maximum was technically lower in most datasets, Copernicus discusses multiple metrics (any one being "lowest" resolves there), and sea ice volume was unambiguously lowest on record.
- **"Other" gets 26%** primarily because NSIDC's "tied" language could be adopted by Copernicus, which would be a classification that isn't cleanly "lowest" or "second-lowest."
- **"Second-lowest" gets 22%** because OSI SAF mid-month data showed daily extent as "second lowest for the date," and the monthly mean may end up above March 2025's record.
- **"No rank" gets 7%** because Copernicus has given explicit rankings for every noteworthy month in their historical pattern.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Which of the following will best match Copernicus’s explicit wording about March 2026 Arctic sea ice in its March 2026 climate bulletin before April 30, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43048)
[Copernicus: Warmest March in Europe and lowest Arctic winter sea ice](https://climate.copernicus.eu/copernicus-warmest-march-europe-and-lowest-arctic-winter-sea-ice)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to the option that matches the explicit wording in the first Copernicus Climate Change Service climate bulletin covering March 2026, published on or linked from https://climate.copernicus.eu/climate-bulletin, before April 30, 2026. If the bulletin explicitly says March 2026 Arctic sea ice was the lowest on record, it resolves to “Lowest on record.” If it explicitly says it was the second-lowest on record, it resolves to “Second-lowest on record.” If it gives any other explicit rank or clear qualitative classification, it resolves to “Other explicit rank or classification.” If it gives no explicit rank or classification for March 2026 Arctic sea ice, it resolves to “No explicit rank or classification stated.”
fine_print: Use only the bulletin's explicit wording. Do not infer a ranking from charts, maps, or raw data if the bulletin does not state one in text. Use the first bulletin covering March 2026, not later retrospective summaries. Candidate Sources: Copernicus climate bulletin index at https://climate.copernicus.eu/climate-bulletin ; the specific March 2026 bulletin page when posted.
